title: Serena Knowledgebase - Orchestration fails when WSDL contains multiple parts (xsds)
content:
Article ID: D12208
Defect ID: DEF199355
Product: SBM 2009 R4
Found In: Service Manager 1.0
Fixed In: SBM 2009 R4.01 Service Manager 1.1

[DESCRIPTION]
Description
If you have an orchestration that calls a webservice for which the WSDL contains multiple parts (i.e. xsd's) then when this web service call is reached in the orchestration the orchestration will fail with an errors similar to the following:
(1) The orchestration actually fails with "Orchestration workflow failed. Contact SBM administrator ....".
(2) The server log contains an error starting with similar to the following:
"00000:OPENARMWorkflow ERROR 2011-03-02 13:54:20,308 org.jbpm.graph.def.GraphElement -- action threw exception: null
java.lang.NullPointerException"
(3) The Common Log will show the following DEBUG error before the orchestration fails as per (1)
WSDLException: faultCode=OTHER_ERROR: Unable to resolve imported document at 'resources/xml/ea449449-8533-41b1-8e66-cc681a28443b/dmwebservices.wsdl'.: This file was not found: file:/C:/Program Files/Serena/SBM/Common/jboss405/bin/resources/xml/ea449449-8533-41b1-8e66-cc681a28443b/dmwebservices.wsdl: java.io.FileNotFoundException: This file was not found: file:/C:/Program Files/Serena/SBM/Common/jboss405/bin/resources/xml/ea449449-8533-41b1-8e66-cc681a28443b/dmwebservices.wsdl

title: Serena Knowledgebase - KM-Dim9:21043-WS_ROOT_HAS_ITEM Error: The Work Set root contains an Item named %s
content:
Article ID: S124525

[DESCRIPTION]
Description
KM-Dim9:21043-WS_ROOT_HAS_ITEM Error: The Work Set root contains an Item named %s

[RESOLUTION]
Resolution
Please gather any applicable information from the list below and contact Dimensions Support for help with this error message.
What you need to troubleshoot:
A screen shot of the full error message. Note: The error message text in your error may vary slightly from this article.
The version and patch level of:
The Dimensions server
The Dimensions server Operating System
The Dimensions client
The Dimensions client Operating System
The Browser (if the error occurred using the Admin Console or Web Client)
The Java in use (if the error occurred using the Admin Console or Web Client)
The IDE used, if applicable
The Database platform and version being used
The exact list of steps to recreate the error message
A detailed control plan report
Any additional details or background information related to this problem
Additional information that may be helpful:
A detailed control plan report
Event logs (Windows OS only)
/var/adm/messages file (Unix Only)
Oracle alert log
Please answer these questions about the problem:
Is the problem reproducible every time? If not, can you determine any factors that cause the error message (ie: the CPU is maxed out when the error occurs)?
Does the problem occur on all machines? If not, is there anything consistent about the machines it does occur on (ie: only windows XP machines)?
Does the problem occur on for all users? If not, is there anything consistent about the users who get the error (ie: is it only users with the developer role)?
How to contact Dimensions Support:
Please use the link below for information about contacting Dimensions Support:
http://support.serena.com/kb/articles/dimensions/kb70712.asp

title: Serena Knowledgebase - VM: PCLI - The project root could not be loaded: "ProjectDBPath" -or- The project root could not be loaded: ""
content:
Article ID: S120118

[DESCRIPTION]
Description
When running a PCLI command the following message is returned:
The project root could not be loaded: "
ProjectDBPath
".

[RESOLUTION]
Resolution
If the error contains a non-empty
ProjectDBPath
then this path is invalid or the Project Database Root is not recognized.
First check the
ProjectDBPath
by opening the Project Database in the GUI and taking note of the path that is specified. This is the path that needs to be passed to the PCLI command, either by using the option
-pr"
ProjectDBPath
"
or by using the environment variable
PCLI_PR
and assigning the value "
ProjectDBPath
" to it.
If the Project Database is using Windows-style paths and PCLI is running on Linux/UNIX, replace all backslash characters (\) with forward slash ones (/) to avoid the shell for treating them as escape characters and mangling the path before it gets to PCLI.
If the Version Manager File Server is used to access your project databases, and other users of that File Server can access the identical
ProjectDBPath
without getting this error, then PCLI may lack the information it needs to locate the File Server. The process that Version Manager uses to locate the File Server is documented in KB doc
S134330
.
Possible culprits that prevent Version Manager from finding the File Server:
Missing File Server definition in
servers.ini
file or not having this file at all.
Missing reference to servers.ini in the file
ISLV.INI
(Windows) or
.islvrc
(Linux/UNIX) or not having this file at all.
Note that on Linux/UNIX every user needs to have his or her own copy of .islvrc in their home directory.
See KB doc
S138903
to learn where Version Manager is looking for the ISLV.INI / .islvrc file.
Users of VM 8.4.4 or older running in setuid mode on Linux/UNIX may also run into a permissions problem. See KB doc
D12816
for more information.
If
ProjectDBPath
in the error message shows up empty, as in:
The project root could not be loaded: "".
then one of the following as happened:
The option
-pr
was not specified and no
PCLI_PR
variable was defined.
The option
-pr
was specified as
─pr
or
—pr
meaning the option does not start with a regular DASH (
"-"
, a.k.a. the minus character) but one of the following:
An EN-DASH
"─"
(Unicode U+2013 / ASCII code 150 or 0x96 in CodePage 1250 / ASCII code 196 or 0xC4 in CodePage 1252)
An EM-DASH "—" (Unicode U+2014 / ASCII code 151 or 0x97 in CodePage 1250)
Although these characters look like a dash they are fundamentally different and not recognized by command line utilities as option lead-in characters.
This can happen when commands are copied from Microsoft Office or Outlook documents, when those tools have been configured to replace certain characters with nicer looking equivalents (dashes, smart quotes, etc.). While the replacement characters may be more pleasing to the eye, they are not functionally equivalent and will cause failures when used from command line tools like PCLI.

[content]
When running a PCLI command the following message is returned:
The project root could not be loaded: "
ProjectDBPath
".

[contentbody]

title: Serena Knowledgebase - SRA: Agent installation fails on linux when AgentOwner is not root
content:
Article ID: S141159

[DESCRIPTION]
Description
Silent install of agent on Linux fails with no error code when the options file contains a non-root user such as the following:
-V AgentOwner="serena"
The installLocation and the IS_DESTINATION were both set to /opt/serena/Release_Automation_Agent, however the "serena" folder did not yet exist.
The log files in the /tmp folder contained the runWrapped log and this indicated an error attempting to invoke the java.
After the installer failed, the Release_Automation_Agent folder and subfolders existed, but the core folder was empty.

[RESOLUTION]
Resolution
The installation script was being run by user root. Therefore, at the start of the installation the folder serena is created underneath of opt. The serena folder is owned by root and the only user with permissions on serena was root.
The Release_Automation_Agent folder was also created. The installer set this to be owned by serena and the permissions were set appropriately on this folder. Because the _jvm folder (underneath Release_Automation_Agent) was still under the path of "serena", the user serena could not access this folder to start the jvm.
To resolve this problem, prior to running the installation, create the serena folder and perform a chmod 755 on this folder. Then invoke the silent installer.

Silent install of agent on Linux fails with no error code when the options file contains a non-root user such as the following:

title: Serena Knowledgebase - Can Tracker point to the PVCSTRK.INI file in a different location than the system root?
content:
Article ID: S119328

[DESCRIPTION]
Description
Setting Tracker and Notify to point to the initialization file (pvcstrk.ini) in a different location than the system root
On certain scenarios, Tracker users do not have permissions on the system root directory (C:\Winnt or C:\Windows) of the machines due to their company's security policy.

Tracker points to the initialization file PVCSTRK.INI file that is created in the system root of the machine while Tracker is installed. This file contains personal settings for Tracker, Tracker Admin, and Notify. Tracker and Notify applications read these settings when they are started.

[RESOLUTION]
Resolution
The initialization file 'pvcstrk.ini' can be copied
from the system root directory to a different location where the Tracker users
have full control permission and, the Tracker, Notify applications can be made
to point to the file in the new location.
Following are the steps to copy the 'pvcstrk.ini' file
from the system root directory to a different location and set Tracker GUI,
Tracker Admin and Notify to point to the file in the new location:
1.
Copy the 'pvcstrk.ini' file from C:\Winnt to another drive (Example: D:\PVCS\newpvcstrk.ini.)
2.
Navigate to Start | Programs | PVCS Tracker | PVCS Tracker.
3.
Right click and select 'Properties'.
4.
The 'Target' option box will have the location of the Tracker executable file
(Example: 'D:\PVCS\Tracker 7.1\Tracker\nt\pvcstkn.exe')
5.
Add the path of the 'newpvcstrk.ini' file to the end of the Tracker executable
path with a '/I' variable (Example: The 'Target' option should have a value like
'D:\PVCS\Tracker 7.1\Tracker\nt\pvcstkn.exe'/I D:\PVCS\newpvcstrk.ini.).
This
will enable the Tracker GUI read the 'newpvcstrk.ini' file.
Repeat
the same steps selecting Tracker Admin in step 2 (Navigate to Start | Programs |
PVCS Tracker | PVCS Tracker Admin). This will enable Tracker Admin read the
'newpvcstrk.ini' file when it is opened.
Also,
follow the same steps selecting PVCS Notify in step 2 (Navigate to Start |
Programs | PVCS Tracker | PVCS Notify). This will enable Notify read the
'newpvcstrk.ini' file when it is opened.

[content]

On certain scenarios, Tracker users do not have permissions on the system root directory (C:\Winnt or C:\Windows) of the machines due to their company's security policy.

Tracker points to the initialization file PVCSTRK.INI file that is created in the system root of the machine while Tracker is installed. This file contains personal settings for Tracker, Tracker Admin, and Notify. Tracker and Notify applications read these settings when they are started.

Description
Setting Tracker and Notify to point to the initialization file (pvcstrk.ini) in a different location than the system root
On certain scenarios, Tracker users do not have permissions on the system root directory (C:\Winnt or C:\Windows) of the machines due to their company's security policy.

Tracker points to the initialization file PVCSTRK.INI file that is created in the system root of the machine while Tracker is installed. This file contains personal settings for Tracker, Tracker Admin, and Notify. Tracker and Notify applications read these settings when they are started.
Resolution
The initialization file 'pvcstrk.ini' can be copied
from the system root directory to a different location where the Tracker users
have full control permission and, the Tracker, Notify applications can be made
to point to the file in the new location.
